title: Failed payments on completed orders
description: Return user_id, name, order_id, payment_method, and amount for orders that are marked as completed but have a payment status of either 'failed' or 'chargeback'. Order by amount descending, then order_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, o.order_id, p.payment_method, p.amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND p.status IN ('failed', 'chargeback')
ORDER BY p.amount DESC, o.order_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, o.order_id, p.payment_method, p.amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND p.status IN ('failed', 'chargeback')
ORDER BY p.amount DESC, o.order_id ASC;
explanation: This query joins users to their orders, then joins those orders to payments so each returned row has both customer and payment context. The two WHERE conditions are important because order status and payment status describe different stages of the transaction lifecycle. Filtering after the joins ensures only completed orders with failed or chargeback payments are returned. The ORDER BY clause makes the output deterministic and highlights the largest problematic payments first.
hints: Join users to orders on user_id, then join orders to payments on order_id.
Filter on both the order status and the payment status.
complexity_hint: O(n) scan with early filter pushdown. The two WHERE conditions reduce the result before sorting.

title: Products in refunded orders
description: Return each distinct product_name and brand for products that appear in orders whose payment_status is 'refunded'. Order by product_name ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT DISTINCT p.product_name, p.brand
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
WHERE o.payment_status = 'refunded'
ORDER BY p.product_name ASC
solution_query:
SELECT DISTINCT p.product_name, p.brand
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
WHERE o.payment_status = 'refunded'
ORDER BY p.product_name ASC;
explanation: The query first links refunded orders to their order items, then links those line items to products so product details can be returned. DISTINCT is necessary because the same product can appear in multiple refunded orders, and the question asks for unique products rather than one row per order item. The WHERE clause applies the business filter at the order level, while ORDER BY makes the final list deterministic and easy to review.
hints: You need orders to find refunded purchases and order_items to reach the product ids.
Use DISTINCT because the same product may appear in more than one refunded order.

title: Departments without employees
description: Return department_id, department_name, and region for departments that do not have any employees assigned to them. Order by department_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
WHERE e.employee_id IS NULL
ORDER BY d.department_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
WHERE e.employee_id IS NULL
ORDER BY d.department_id ASC;
explanation: A LEFT JOIN keeps every department even when there is no matching employee row. After the join, departments with no employees will have NULL values from the employees table, so filtering on e.employee_id IS NULL isolates exactly those unmatched departments. This is a standard medium-level join pattern because it depends on understanding how NULLs behave after an outer join. ORDER BY department_id keeps the result deterministic.
hints: Use a LEFT JOIN so departments still appear even when they have no matching employee rows.
After the join, look for NULL on a column from the employees table.
complexity_hint: LEFT JOIN anti-pattern: keep rows from the left table that have no match on the right. Can also be written with NOT IN or NOT EXISTS — LEFT JOIN + IS NULL is typically most performant.

alternative_solution.query:
SELECT d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region
FROM departments d
WHERE d.department_id NOT IN (
  SELECT department_id FROM employees
)
ORDER BY d.department_id ASC
alternative_solution.explanation: This alternative uses a NOT IN subquery instead of LEFT JOIN + IS NULL. Both produce identical results here, but NOT IN can silently return no rows if the subquery produces any NULLs — use LEFT JOIN + IS NULL in production for safety.

title: Paid revenue by user country
description: Return each country and the total paid payment amount for completed orders from users in that country as total_paid_amount. Order by total_paid_amount descending, then country ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.country, SUM(p.amount) AS total_paid_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND p.status = 'paid'
GROUP BY u.country
ORDER BY total_paid_amount DESC, u.country ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.country, SUM(p.amount) AS total_paid_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND p.status = 'paid'
GROUP BY u.country
ORDER BY total_paid_amount DESC, u.country ASC;
explanation: This query requires joining before aggregating because the country lives on users, the order completion state lives on orders, and the payment amount lives on payments. The WHERE clause narrows the data to completed orders with paid payments before the SUM is calculated. GROUP BY country then rolls those filtered rows up to one revenue total per country. ORDER BY surfaces the highest-revenue countries first and guarantees deterministic output.
hints: You need three tables because the country, order state, and payment amount are stored separately.
Filter first, then group by country and sum the payment amount.
complexity_hint: O(n log n) due to GROUP BY sort. Filtering with WHERE before aggregation is the key to keeping this efficient.

title: Country-plan segments with high average order value
description: Return country, plan_tier, and the average net_amount of completed orders as avg_completed_amount for country and plan tier combinations whose average completed order value is greater than 600. Order by avg_completed_amount descending, then country ascending, then plan_tier ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.country, u.plan_tier, AVG(o.net_amount) AS avg_completed_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY u.country, u.plan_tier
HAVING AVG(o.net_amount) > 600
ORDER BY avg_completed_amount DESC, u.country ASC, u.plan_tier ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.country, u.plan_tier, AVG(o.net_amount) AS avg_completed_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY u.country, u.plan_tier
HAVING AVG(o.net_amount) > 600
ORDER BY avg_completed_amount DESC, u.country ASC, u.plan_tier ASC;
explanation: The join links each order to user attributes so the aggregation can happen at the country and plan tier level. WHERE filters the dataset down to completed orders before grouping, which ensures cancelled orders do not affect the average. GROUP BY on two columns creates a segment-level view, and HAVING filters those aggregated segments after AVG has been computed. This is exactly the kind of medium reasoning where WHERE and HAVING must be used for different purposes.
hints: Group by both country and plan_tier because the result is at the segment level.
Use HAVING, not WHERE, to keep only groups whose average net_amount is above 600.

title: Users with multiple paid payment methods
description: Return user_id, name, and the number of distinct payment methods used across paid payments as payment_method_count. Include only users who have used more than one distinct payment method. Order by payment_method_count descending, then user_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_method) AS payment_method_count
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE p.status = 'paid'
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_method) > 1
ORDER BY payment_method_count DESC, u.user_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_method) AS payment_method_count
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE p.status = 'paid'
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_method) > 1
ORDER BY payment_method_count DESC, u.user_id ASC;
explanation: This query joins users to orders and then to payments so payment method behavior can be analyzed per user. COUNT(DISTINCT payment_method) is important because repeated use of the same method should count once, not once per payment row. WHERE limits the analysis to paid payments before the grouping happens. HAVING then filters the grouped results to only users who truly used more than one distinct payment method.
hints: You need DISTINCT inside COUNT because the question is about unique payment methods, not raw payment rows.
Use HAVING after GROUP BY to keep only users whose distinct payment method count is greater than 1.

title: Slow resolved ticket groups
description: Return priority, issue_type, and the average resolution_hours for resolved tickets as avg_resolution_hours. Include only priority and issue type groups whose average resolution_hours is greater than 72. Order by avg_resolution_hours descending, then priority ascending, then issue_type ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT priority, issue_type, AVG(resolution_hours) AS avg_resolution_hours
FROM support_tickets
WHERE status = 'resolved'
GROUP BY priority, issue_type
HAVING AVG(resolution_hours) > 72
ORDER BY avg_resolution_hours DESC, priority ASC, issue_type ASC
solution_query:
SELECT priority, issue_type, AVG(resolution_hours) AS avg_resolution_hours
FROM support_tickets
WHERE status = 'resolved'
GROUP BY priority, issue_type
HAVING AVG(resolution_hours) > 72
ORDER BY avg_resolution_hours DESC, priority ASC, issue_type ASC;
explanation: The WHERE clause limits the input rows to resolved tickets because unresolved tickets should not affect resolution-time analysis. GROUP BY priority and issue_type creates a separate aggregate for each operational category. HAVING then removes groups whose average resolution time is not high enough to be considered slow. This structure demonstrates the medium-level difference between filtering raw rows first and filtering aggregated groups later.
hints: Filter to resolved tickets before you calculate the average resolution time.
Because the filter is on an aggregate, use HAVING rather than WHERE.

title: Order value bands
description: Bucket orders into three bands based on net_amount: 'low value' for amounts under 100, 'mid value' for amounts from 100 up to but not including 500, and 'high value' for amounts of 500 or more. Return order_value_band and order_count. Order by order_count descending, then order_value_band ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT CASE
 WHEN net_amount < 100 THEN 'low value'
 WHEN net_amount < 500 THEN 'mid value'
 ELSE 'high value'
 END AS order_value_band,
 COUNT(order_id) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY CASE
 WHEN net_amount < 100 THEN 'low value'
 WHEN net_amount < 500 THEN 'mid value'
 ELSE 'high value'
 END
ORDER BY order_count DESC, order_value_band ASC
solution_query:
SELECT CASE
 WHEN net_amount < 100 THEN 'low value'
 WHEN net_amount < 500 THEN 'mid value'
 ELSE 'high value'
 END AS order_value_band,
 COUNT(order_id) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY CASE
 WHEN net_amount < 100 THEN 'low value'
 WHEN net_amount < 500 THEN 'mid value'
 ELSE 'high value'
 END
ORDER BY order_count DESC, order_value_band ASC;
explanation: CASE WHEN creates a derived label for each order based on its net_amount, which turns a continuous numeric value into business-friendly categories. Because the output is summarized by those labels, the same CASE expression must appear in the GROUP BY clause. COUNT then measures how many orders fall into each band. The ORDER BY clause makes the summary deterministic and shows the most common band first.
hints: Use CASE WHEN to turn net_amount into a text bucket.
Group by the same CASE expression you use in the SELECT list.

title: Salary bands by department region
description: Return region, salary_band, and employee_count, where salary_band is 'high salary' for salaries of 100000 or more and 'standard salary' otherwise. Count employees per region and salary_band. Order by region ascending, then salary_band ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT d.region,
 CASE WHEN e.salary >= 100000 THEN 'high salary' ELSE 'standard salary' END AS salary_band,
 COUNT(e.employee_id) AS employee_count
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
GROUP BY d.region,
 CASE WHEN e.salary >= 100000 THEN 'high salary' ELSE 'standard salary' END
ORDER BY d.region ASC, salary_band ASC
solution_query:
SELECT d.region,
 CASE WHEN e.salary >= 100000 THEN 'high salary' ELSE 'standard salary' END AS salary_band,
 COUNT(e.employee_id) AS employee_count
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
GROUP BY d.region,
 CASE WHEN e.salary >= 100000 THEN 'high salary' ELSE 'standard salary' END
ORDER BY d.region ASC, salary_band ASC;
explanation: The join is needed because the salary information is stored on employees while region is stored on departments. CASE WHEN derives a salary band for each employee row before aggregation. GROUP BY on region and the derived band produces one count per regional bucket. This introduces medium-level reasoning because the result depends on both a relationship and a computed category rather than a direct column group.
hints: Join employees to departments first so each employee row has a region.
Use CASE WHEN to create the salary_band, then group by both region and that derived band.

title: Users with chargeback orders
description: Return user_id and name for users who have at least one order that appears in the payments table with status = 'chargeback'. Order by user_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name
FROM users u
WHERE u.user_id IN (
 SELECT o.user_id
 FROM orders o
 JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
 WHERE p.status = 'chargeback'
)
ORDER BY u.user_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name
FROM users u
WHERE u.user_id IN (
 SELECT o.user_id
 FROM orders o
 JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
 WHERE p.status = 'chargeback'
)
ORDER BY u.user_id ASC;
explanation: The subquery first identifies which user_ids are associated with at least one chargeback payment by joining orders to payments. The outer query then filters the users table to only those matching user_ids. This is a good introductory subquery pattern because the inner query solves a narrower set-membership problem and the outer query uses that result to fetch descriptive user columns. ORDER BY user_id keeps the final output deterministic.
hints: Use a subquery to identify the user_ids tied to chargeback payments.
The outer query should filter users based on whether their user_id appears in that inner result set.

title: Paid completed orders by country
description: Return one row per country with columns country, paid_completed_order_count, and total_paid_amount. Count only orders where the order status is 'completed' and there is at least one payment row with payment status 'paid'. If an order has multiple paid payment rows, it should still count as one order in paid_completed_order_count. Sum the paid payment amounts across qualifying payment rows. Order by total_paid_amount descending, then country ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.country, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS paid_completed_order_count, SUM(p.amount) AS total_paid_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND p.status = 'paid'
GROUP BY u.country
ORDER BY total_paid_amount DESC, u.country ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.country, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS paid_completed_order_count, SUM(p.amount) AS total_paid_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND p.status = 'paid'
GROUP BY u.country
ORDER BY total_paid_amount DESC, u.country ASC;
explanation: This query joins users to orders and payments so each payment can be tied back to a user country. The WHERE clause filters to completed orders and paid payment rows before any aggregation happens, which keeps only the business events we care about. Because one order can have multiple paid payment rows, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) is necessary to avoid counting the same order multiple times. SUM(p.amount) then adds the qualifying payment amounts, and the final ORDER BY makes the output deterministic.
hints: Filter completed orders and paid payments before grouping.
An order can match multiple payment rows, so think about how to count orders only once.
Group by country after the joins.

title: Brand reach in completed 2024 orders
description: For each product brand, return brand, distinct_order_count, and total_units_sold for completed orders placed in 2024. distinct_order_count should count an order only once per brand, even if that order contains multiple line items from the same brand. total_units_sold should sum quantity across those qualifying line items. Order by total_units_sold descending, then brand ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT p.brand, COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) AS distinct_order_count, SUM(oi.quantity) AS total_units_sold
FROM products p
JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND o.order_date < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY p.brand
ORDER BY total_units_sold DESC, p.brand ASC
solution_query:
SELECT p.brand, COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) AS distinct_order_count, SUM(oi.quantity) AS total_units_sold
FROM products p
JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND o.order_date < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY p.brand
ORDER BY total_units_sold DESC, p.brand ASC;
explanation: The query starts by joining products to order_items so each line item is associated with a brand, then joins orders so order-level filters can be applied. The WHERE clause limits rows to completed orders from 2024 before aggregation, which prevents unrelated rows from affecting the totals. COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) counts each qualifying order once per brand, even when multiple items from the same brand appear in that order. SUM(oi.quantity) measures total units sold, and the result is sorted deterministically.
hints: You need product details from one table and order status/date from another.
Count orders at the brand level without double-counting repeated line items in the same order.
Apply the 2024 and completed-order filters before grouping.

title: Users with both orders and high-priority tickets
description: Return one row per country with columns country and affected_user_count. A user should be counted only if they have at least one completed order and at least one support ticket with priority = 'high'. Count each qualifying user once per country, even if they have many completed orders and many high-priority tickets. Order by affected_user_count descending, then country ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.country, COUNT(DISTINCT u.user_id) AS affected_user_count
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN support_tickets st ON u.user_id = st.user_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND st.priority = 'high'
GROUP BY u.country
ORDER BY affected_user_count DESC, u.country ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.country, COUNT(DISTINCT u.user_id) AS affected_user_count
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN support_tickets st ON u.user_id = st.user_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND st.priority = 'high'
GROUP BY u.country
ORDER BY affected_user_count DESC, u.country ASC;
explanation: This question requires combining two different user activities: purchasing and support engagement. Joining users to both orders and support_tickets creates one row for each matching order-ticket combination, which can multiply rows for the same user. COUNT(DISTINCT u.user_id) is therefore required to avoid inflated counts. The filters for completed orders and high-priority tickets belong in the WHERE clause because they determine which joined rows qualify before aggregation.
hints: A user must satisfy conditions from both the orders table and the support_tickets table.
Joining both activity tables can create duplicate user rows.
Count users, not rows.

title: Employees above their department average
description: Return employee_id, employee_name, department_name, and salary for employees whose salary is strictly greater than the average salary of their own department. Order by department_name ascending, then salary descending, then employee_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT e.employee_id, e.employee_name, d.department_name, e.salary
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
WHERE e.salary > (
 SELECT AVG(e2.salary)
 FROM employees e2
 WHERE e2.department_id = e.department_id
)
ORDER BY d.department_name ASC, e.salary DESC, e.employee_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT e.employee_id, e.employee_name, d.department_name, e.salary
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
WHERE e.salary > (
 SELECT AVG(e2.salary)
 FROM employees e2
 WHERE e2.department_id = e.department_id
)
ORDER BY d.department_name ASC, e.salary DESC, e.employee_id ASC;
explanation: The outer query returns employee details along with the department name. The correlated subquery recalculates the average salary for the current employee's department by matching e2.department_id to e.department_id. Because that subquery depends on the outer row, it produces a department-specific comparison instead of one global average. The WHERE clause keeps only employees whose salary is above their own department average, and the ORDER BY produces a stable result.
hints: You need to compare each employee to a department-specific average, not one average for the whole company.
The subquery should reference the outer employee row through department_id.
Join departments only to display the department name.

title: Early signups with no completed orders
description: Return user_id, name, and country for users who signed up before 2024-01-01 and have never placed a completed order. Order by user_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, u.country
FROM users u
WHERE u.signup_date < '2024-01-01'
 AND NOT EXISTS (
 SELECT 1
 FROM orders o
 WHERE o.user_id = u.user_id
 AND o.status = 'completed'
 )
ORDER BY u.user_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, u.country
FROM users u
WHERE u.signup_date < '2024-01-01'
 AND NOT EXISTS (
 SELECT 1
 FROM orders o
 WHERE o.user_id = u.user_id
 AND o.status = 'completed'
 )
ORDER BY u.user_id ASC;
explanation: The outer query starts from users because the result needs user-level output, including people who may have no qualifying orders at all. NOT EXISTS is the correct anti-subquery pattern here because it checks whether any completed order is present for the current user. If none exists, that user remains in the result. The signup_date filter is applied in the outer WHERE clause so only early signups are evaluated, and the final ordering is deterministic.
hints: Start from the users table because you need to keep users even when matching orders do not exist.
Use an anti-subquery pattern to exclude users who have at least one completed order.
Apply the signup-date filter in the outer query.

title: Brands above average completed-order revenue
description: Return brand and total_revenue for brands whose revenue from completed orders is greater than the average brand revenue from completed orders across all brands. Order by total_revenue descending, then brand ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT p.brand, SUM(oi.line_amount) AS total_revenue
FROM products p
JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.brand
HAVING SUM(oi.line_amount) > (
 SELECT AVG(brand_revenue)
 FROM (
 SELECT SUM(oi2.line_amount) AS brand_revenue
 FROM products p2
 JOIN order_items oi2 ON p2.product_id = oi2.product_id
 JOIN orders o2 ON oi2.order_id = o2.order_id
 WHERE o2.status = 'completed'
 GROUP BY p2.brand
 ) revenue_by_brand
)
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC, p.brand ASC
solution_query:
SELECT p.brand, SUM(oi.line_amount) AS total_revenue
FROM products p
JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.brand
HAVING SUM(oi.line_amount) > (
 SELECT AVG(brand_revenue)
 FROM (
 SELECT SUM(oi2.line_amount) AS brand_revenue
 FROM products p2
 JOIN order_items oi2 ON p2.product_id = oi2.product_id
 JOIN orders o2 ON oi2.order_id = o2.order_id
 WHERE o2.status = 'completed'
 GROUP BY p2.brand
 ) revenue_by_brand
)
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC, p.brand ASC;
explanation: The outer query calculates total completed-order revenue per brand. The subquery in the HAVING clause computes the average of those brand-level revenue totals by first aggregating revenue per brand in a derived table and then averaging those results. This is different from averaging individual line items, which would answer a different question. HAVING is required because the comparison happens after the outer query has grouped rows by brand.
hints: First think about how to compute revenue per brand from completed orders.
The comparison target is an average of brand totals, not an average of individual order lines.
Use HAVING because you are comparing an aggregate result.

title: Country order outcome mix
description: Return country, completed_orders, and cancelled_orders for countries with at least 50 total orders. Count orders from the orders table joined to users. Order by completed_orders descending, then country ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.country,
 SUM(CASE WHEN o.status = 'completed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS completed_orders,
 SUM(CASE WHEN o.status = 'cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled_orders
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
GROUP BY u.country
HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) >= 50
ORDER BY completed_orders DESC, u.country ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.country,
 SUM(CASE WHEN o.status = 'completed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS completed_orders,
 SUM(CASE WHEN o.status = 'cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled_orders
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
GROUP BY u.country
HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) >= 50
ORDER BY completed_orders DESC, u.country ASC;
explanation: This query uses conditional aggregation to produce two different counts from the same grouped set of order rows. After joining orders to users, rows are grouped by country. Each SUM(CASE WHEN ...) counts only the rows that match one status condition, while HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) >= 50 removes countries with too little activity. This pattern is useful when several related metrics need to be computed in a single grouped query.
hints: You need multiple counts from the same grouped rows, not separate queries.
Use CASE inside an aggregate to count only rows with one status at a time.
Apply the minimum-total-orders rule with HAVING.

title: Ticket resolution mix by issue type
description: Return issue_type, resolved_ticket_count, unresolved_ticket_count, and avg_resolved_hours. unresolved_ticket_count should include tickets whose status is 'open' or 'in_progress'. avg_resolved_hours should average resolution_hours only for resolved tickets. Include only issue types with at least 20 total tickets. Order by resolved_ticket_count descending, then issue_type ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT issue_type,
 SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'resolved' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS resolved_ticket_count,
 SUM(CASE WHEN status IN ('open', 'in_progress') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS unresolved_ticket_count,
 AVG(CASE WHEN status = 'resolved' THEN resolution_hours END) AS avg_resolved_hours
FROM support_tickets
GROUP BY issue_type
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 20
ORDER BY resolved_ticket_count DESC, issue_type ASC
solution_query:
SELECT issue_type,
 SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'resolved' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS resolved_ticket_count,
 SUM(CASE WHEN status IN ('open', 'in_progress') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS unresolved_ticket_count,
 AVG(CASE WHEN status = 'resolved' THEN resolution_hours END) AS avg_resolved_hours
FROM support_tickets
GROUP BY issue_type
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 20
ORDER BY resolved_ticket_count DESC, issue_type ASC;
explanation: The query groups tickets by issue_type and then calculates multiple metrics from that same grouped data. Conditional SUM expressions separate resolved from unresolved tickets without needing multiple passes over the table. AVG(CASE WHEN status = 'resolved' THEN resolution_hours END) averages only resolved-ticket times because unresolved rows contribute NULL, which AVG ignores. HAVING enforces the minimum volume requirement after the grouping step.
hints: Use one grouped query to produce all four output columns.
A CASE expression inside AVG can limit which rows contribute to the average.
The minimum-ticket requirement belongs after grouping.

title: Repeat large-order users in selected countries
description: Return user_id, name, completed_order_count, and avg_net_amount for users from US, UK, or CA who placed at least 3 completed orders in 2024 where each counted order has net_amount >= 500. Order by avg_net_amount descending, then user_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, COUNT(o.order_id) AS completed_order_count, AVG(o.net_amount) AS avg_net_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
WHERE u.country IN ('US', 'UK', 'CA')
 AND o.status = 'completed'
 AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND o.order_date < '2025-01-01'
 AND o.net_amount >= 500
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name
HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) >= 3
ORDER BY avg_net_amount DESC, u.user_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, COUNT(o.order_id) AS completed_order_count, AVG(o.net_amount) AS avg_net_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
WHERE u.country IN ('US', 'UK', 'CA')
 AND o.status = 'completed'
 AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND o.order_date < '2025-01-01'
 AND o.net_amount >= 500
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name
HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) >= 3
ORDER BY avg_net_amount DESC, u.user_id ASC;
explanation: This problem combines row-level filters with group-level filters. The WHERE clause first limits rows to selected countries and qualifying orders in 2024 with sufficient net_amount. After those rows are filtered, the query groups by user and calculates both the order count and the average net amount. HAVING is then used to keep only users who still have at least 3 qualifying orders after all row-level filters have been applied.
hints: Decide which conditions should reduce rows before grouping and which should filter grouped results after aggregation.
The order count threshold must be evaluated after grouping by user.
Average only the orders that passed the earlier filters.

title: High-volume brands in completed 2024 orders
description: Return category_id, brand, distinct_order_count, and total_units for product brands that meet all of the following conditions: the product launch_date is not null and earlier than 2025-01-01, the order is completed, the order was placed in 2024, the brand appears in at least 25 distinct qualifying orders, and the brand sold at least 80 total units across those qualifying rows. Order by distinct_order_count descending, then category_id ascending, then brand ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT p.category_id, p.brand, COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) AS distinct_order_count, SUM(oi.quantity) AS total_units
FROM products p
JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
WHERE p.launch_date IS NOT NULL
 AND p.launch_date < '2025-01-01'
 AND o.status = 'completed'
 AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND o.order_date < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY p.category_id, p.brand
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) >= 25
 AND SUM(oi.quantity) >= 80
ORDER BY distinct_order_count DESC, p.category_id ASC, p.brand ASC
solution_query:
SELECT p.category_id, p.brand, COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) AS distinct_order_count, SUM(oi.quantity) AS total_units
FROM products p
JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
WHERE p.launch_date IS NOT NULL
 AND p.launch_date < '2025-01-01'
 AND o.status = 'completed'
 AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND o.order_date < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY p.category_id, p.brand
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) >= 25
 AND SUM(oi.quantity) >= 80
ORDER BY distinct_order_count DESC, p.category_id ASC, p.brand ASC;
explanation: This query requires filtering at multiple stages. The WHERE clause first removes products and orders that do not meet the launch-date, order-status, and date-range requirements, so only relevant detail rows remain. The grouped result is then produced at the category-and-brand level, where COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) measures how many qualifying orders included that brand and SUM(oi.quantity) measures total units sold. HAVING applies the volume thresholds after those grouped metrics have been computed.
hints: Use WHERE for the product and order filters that apply before aggregation.
Group by both category_id and brand because both appear in the output.
The minimum order-count and unit thresholds belong in HAVING.

title: Departments with no 2024 hires
description: Return department_id, department_name, and hires_2024 for departments that had zero employees hired during 2024. hires_2024 should appear as 0 for every returned row. Order by department_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT d.department_id, d.department_name, COUNT(e.employee_id) AS hires_2024
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e
  ON d.department_id = e.department_id
 AND e.hire_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND e.hire_date < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY d.department_id, d.department_name
HAVING COUNT(e.employee_id) = 0
ORDER BY d.department_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT d.department_id, d.department_name, COUNT(e.employee_id) AS hires_2024
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e
  ON d.department_id = e.department_id
 AND e.hire_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND e.hire_date < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY d.department_id, d.department_name
HAVING COUNT(e.employee_id) = 0
ORDER BY d.department_id ASC;
explanation: This query starts from departments because departments with no matching hires still need to appear in the result. The date filter belongs inside the LEFT JOIN condition, not in the WHERE clause, so departments with no 2024 hires are preserved instead of being filtered out. After grouping by department, COUNT(e.employee_id) counts only matched 2024 hires, and HAVING COUNT(e.employee_id) = 0 keeps only departments with none. The final ORDER BY makes the output deterministic.
hints: Start from departments, not employees, because departments with no matching hires must still be considered.
If you put the hire-date filter in WHERE, the LEFT JOIN will behave like an INNER JOIN.
Use HAVING after grouping to keep only zero-hire departments.

title: Selected-country users without 2024 orders
description: Return user_id, name, and country for users from US, UK, or CA who placed no orders during 2024, regardless of order status. Order by user_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, u.country
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o
  ON u.user_id = o.user_id
 AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND o.order_date < '2025-01-01'
WHERE u.country IN ('US', 'UK', 'CA')
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name, u.country
HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) = 0
ORDER BY u.user_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, u.country
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o
  ON u.user_id = o.user_id
 AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND o.order_date < '2025-01-01'
WHERE u.country IN ('US', 'UK', 'CA')
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name, u.country
HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) = 0
ORDER BY u.user_id ASC;
explanation: The result must include users even when they have no 2024 orders, so the query starts from users and uses a LEFT JOIN to orders. The 2024 date filter is applied in the join condition so only 2024 orders can match, while users with no such orders remain in the joined result. The country filter belongs in WHERE because it applies to the driving users table. After grouping by user, COUNT(o.order_id) counts only matched 2024 orders, and HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) = 0 keeps the users with none. This is a join-correctness problem because moving the order-date filter to WHERE would incorrectly remove the users you need.
hints: Use users as the driving table because users with no matching 2024 orders must stay visible.
Filter the user list to the requested countries before applying the zero-order test.
Filter the order date range before the grouping, but be careful where that filter is placed.

title: Completed 2024 orders missing paid payments
description: Return order_id, user_id, and order_status for completed orders placed in 2024 that have no payment row with status = 'paid'. Order by order_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT o.order_id, o.user_id, o.status AS order_status
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN payments p
  ON o.order_id = p.order_id
 AND p.status = 'paid'
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND o.order_date < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY o.order_id, o.user_id, o.status
HAVING COUNT(p.payment_id) = 0
ORDER BY o.order_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT o.order_id, o.user_id, o.status AS order_status
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN payments p
  ON o.order_id = p.order_id
 AND p.status = 'paid'
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND o.order_date < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY o.order_id, o.user_id, o.status
HAVING COUNT(p.payment_id) = 0
ORDER BY o.order_id ASC;
explanation: This query checks for completed 2024 orders that never matched a paid payment row. The payment-status filter belongs in the LEFT JOIN condition so each order is tested specifically for a paid payment match. If the filter were moved into WHERE, orders without a paid payment would disappear before the final check. Grouping by order lets COUNT(p.payment_id) count only matching paid payments, and HAVING COUNT(p.payment_id) = 0 keeps the orders with none.
hints: You need every qualifying order to remain visible even when no paid payment row exists.
Join only to paid payments, then test which orders matched zero rows.
Group by the order columns before applying the zero-match condition.

title: Category launch-date completeness snapshot
description: For each category, return category_id, category_name, launched_product_count, missing_launch_date_count, and avg_launched_price. launched_product_count should count products whose launch_date is not null. avg_launched_price should average price only for products whose launch_date is not null. Include only categories with at least 8 total products. Order by category_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT c.category_id, c.category_name,
 SUM(CASE WHEN p.launch_date IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS launched_product_count,
 SUM(CASE WHEN p.launch_date IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS missing_launch_date_count,
 AVG(CASE WHEN p.launch_date IS NOT NULL THEN p.price END) AS avg_launched_price
FROM categories c
JOIN products p ON c.category_id = p.category_id
GROUP BY c.category_id, c.category_name
HAVING COUNT(p.product_id) >= 8
ORDER BY c.category_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT c.category_id, c.category_name,
 SUM(CASE WHEN p.launch_date IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS launched_product_count,
 SUM(CASE WHEN p.launch_date IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS missing_launch_date_count,
 AVG(CASE WHEN p.launch_date IS NOT NULL THEN p.price END) AS avg_launched_price
FROM categories c
JOIN products p ON c.category_id = p.category_id
GROUP BY c.category_id, c.category_name
HAVING COUNT(p.product_id) >= 8
ORDER BY c.category_id ASC;
explanation: This is an aggregation edge-case question because one grouped result needs several metrics with different row-participation rules. The CASE expressions count launched and missing-launch-date products separately inside the same grouped query. AVG(CASE WHEN p.launch_date IS NOT NULL THEN p.price END) averages only the products with a known launch date because the others contribute NULL, which AVG ignores. GROUP BY is required for category-level output, and HAVING enforces the minimum total-product threshold after grouping.
hints: Use one grouped query to compute all category-level metrics.
CASE inside SUM can count rows with and without launch dates separately.
To average only launched products, let missing launch dates contribute NULL to AVG.

title: Country and plan tier order mix
description: Return country, plan_tier, completed_order_count, and cancelled_order_count for user segments with at least 25 total orders. Count order rows after joining users to orders. Order by country ascending, then plan_tier ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.country, u.plan_tier,
 SUM(CASE WHEN o.status = 'completed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS completed_order_count,
 SUM(CASE WHEN o.status = 'cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled_order_count
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
GROUP BY u.country, u.plan_tier
HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) >= 25
ORDER BY u.country ASC, u.plan_tier ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.country, u.plan_tier,
 SUM(CASE WHEN o.status = 'completed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS completed_order_count,
 SUM(CASE WHEN o.status = 'cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled_order_count
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
GROUP BY u.country, u.plan_tier
HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) >= 25
ORDER BY u.country ASC, u.plan_tier ASC;
explanation: The core trap here is grouping at the right grain. Because the output is segmented by both country and plan_tier, both columns must appear in GROUP BY. Conditional aggregation then computes the completed and cancelled counts from the same grouped order rows. HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) >= 25 removes segments with too little activity only after the grouped totals have been calculated. This increases reasoning complexity without using more advanced SQL features.
hints: Match the grouping columns to the exact business segment named in the output.
Use CASE inside SUM to split one set of order rows into status-specific counts.
The minimum-order rule should be applied after grouping.

title: Department hiring cohort summary
description: Return department_name, recent_hire_count, older_hire_count, and avg_recent_hire_salary for departments with at least 10 employees total. A recent hire is an employee with hire_date on or after 2023-01-01. avg_recent_hire_salary should average salary only for recent hires. Order by recent_hire_count descending, then department_name ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT d.department_name,
 SUM(CASE WHEN e.hire_date >= '2023-01-01' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS recent_hire_count,
 SUM(CASE WHEN e.hire_date < '2023-01-01' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS older_hire_count,
 AVG(CASE WHEN e.hire_date >= '2023-01-01' THEN e.salary END) AS avg_recent_hire_salary
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
GROUP BY d.department_name
HAVING COUNT(e.employee_id) >= 10
ORDER BY recent_hire_count DESC, d.department_name ASC
solution_query:
SELECT d.department_name,
 SUM(CASE WHEN e.hire_date >= '2023-01-01' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS recent_hire_count,
 SUM(CASE WHEN e.hire_date < '2023-01-01' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS older_hire_count,
 AVG(CASE WHEN e.hire_date >= '2023-01-01' THEN e.salary END) AS avg_recent_hire_salary
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
GROUP BY d.department_name
HAVING COUNT(e.employee_id) >= 10
ORDER BY recent_hire_count DESC, d.department_name ASC;
explanation: This query combines conditional aggregation with a grouped staffing threshold. After joining departments to employees, the grouped rows are split into recent and older hires using separate CASE expressions inside SUM. AVG(CASE WHEN e.hire_date >= '2023-01-01' THEN e.salary END) calculates a recent-hire-only average because older hires contribute NULL, which AVG ignores. HAVING ensures that only departments with enough total employees are included, while the ORDER BY sorts by the recent-hire volume first.
hints: Use one grouped query per department rather than separate queries for recent and older hires.
CASE inside AVG can limit which employee salaries contribute to the average.
The minimum total-employee condition depends on the full grouped set, not just recent hires.

title: Products never sold in completed orders
description: Return product_id, product_name, and brand for products launched before 2024-01-01 that have never appeared in an order_item tied to a completed order. Order by product_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT p.product_id, p.product_name, p.brand
FROM products p
WHERE p.launch_date < '2024-01-01'
 AND NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM order_items oi
  JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
  WHERE oi.product_id = p.product_id
   AND o.status = 'completed'
 )
ORDER BY p.product_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT p.product_id, p.product_name, p.brand
FROM products p
WHERE p.launch_date < '2024-01-01'
 AND NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM order_items oi
  JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
  WHERE oi.product_id = p.product_id
   AND o.status = 'completed'
 )
ORDER BY p.product_id ASC;
explanation: The outer query starts from products because the result should include products that might have no matching order history at all. The NOT EXISTS subquery checks whether a completed-order match exists for the current product by joining order_items to orders and correlating back through oi.product_id = p.product_id. If no such completed-order row exists, the product stays in the result. This is a clean anti-match pattern that avoids accidental loss of products with no order-item rows.
hints: Start from products because products with no completed-order history must still be testable.
The subquery should look for evidence that a product appears in a completed order.
Keep only products for which that evidence does not exist.

title: Departments above their region average headcount
description: Return department_id, department_name, region, and employee_count for departments whose employee_count is strictly greater than the average department employee_count within the same region. Include departments with zero employees when calculating each region average. Order by region ascending, then employee_count descending, then department_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region, COUNT(e.employee_id) AS employee_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
GROUP BY d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region
HAVING COUNT(e.employee_id) > (
  SELECT AVG(region_department_count)
  FROM (
    SELECT COUNT(e2.employee_id) AS region_department_count
    FROM departments d2
    LEFT JOIN employees e2 ON d2.department_id = e2.department_id
    WHERE d2.region = d.region
    GROUP BY d2.department_id
  ) region_counts
)
ORDER BY d.region ASC, employee_count DESC, d.department_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region, COUNT(e.employee_id) AS employee_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
GROUP BY d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region
HAVING COUNT(e.employee_id) > (
  SELECT AVG(region_department_count)
  FROM (
    SELECT COUNT(e2.employee_id) AS region_department_count
    FROM departments d2
    LEFT JOIN employees e2 ON d2.department_id = e2.department_id
    WHERE d2.region = d.region
    GROUP BY d2.department_id
  ) region_counts
)
ORDER BY d.region ASC, employee_count DESC, d.department_id ASC;
explanation: The outer query computes one employee_count per department, including departments with zero employees because of the LEFT JOIN. The correlated subquery then calculates the average department headcount for the current outer region by building a region-specific list of department counts and averaging them. Including zero-employee departments in that inner grouped set matters because the description explicitly says they must affect the regional average. The HAVING comparison happens after the outer department count has been computed, which is why this is a nested aggregation subquery pattern.
hints: First compute one employee count per department in the outer query.
The comparison target is not a single global average; it changes with the outer row's region.
Make sure zero-employee departments still participate in the regional average calculation.

title: Users with repeated refunds and no open tickets
description: Return user_id, name, refunded_payment_count, and refunded_amount for users who have at least 2 payment rows with status = 'refunded' and have no support ticket whose status is 'open' or 'in_progress'. Order by refunded_amount descending, then user_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, COUNT(p.payment_id) AS refunded_payment_count, SUM(p.amount) AS refunded_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE p.status = 'refunded'
 AND NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM support_tickets st
  WHERE st.user_id = u.user_id
   AND st.status IN ('open', 'in_progress')
 )
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name
HAVING COUNT(p.payment_id) >= 2
ORDER BY refunded_amount DESC, u.user_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, COUNT(p.payment_id) AS refunded_payment_count, SUM(p.amount) AS refunded_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE p.status = 'refunded'
 AND NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM support_tickets st
  WHERE st.user_id = u.user_id
   AND st.status IN ('open', 'in_progress')
 )
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name
HAVING COUNT(p.payment_id) >= 2
ORDER BY refunded_amount DESC, u.user_id ASC;
explanation: This is a business-logic question that combines payment behavior with support status. The main query joins users to their orders and payment rows, then filters to refunded payment rows only. The NOT EXISTS subquery removes any user who currently has an open or in-progress support ticket, which keeps the user-level business rule separate from the payment-row aggregation. After grouping by user, HAVING COUNT(p.payment_id) >= 2 enforces the repeated-refund threshold, and SUM(p.amount) measures the refund exposure.
hints: Aggregate refunded payment rows at the user level.
Handle the support-ticket exclusion as a separate user-level check, not by joining ticket rows into the main aggregation.
Use HAVING for the minimum refund-count requirement.

title: Countries with problematic payment totals above paid totals
description: Return country, paid_amount, and problematic_amount for countries where completed orders placed in 2024 produced more problematic payment amount than paid payment amount. Treat problematic payments as rows whose payment status is 'failed' or 'refunded'. Include only countries with at least 20 payment rows tied to completed 2024 orders. Order by problematic_amount descending, then country ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.country,
 SUM(CASE WHEN p.status = 'paid' THEN p.amount ELSE 0 END) AS paid_amount,
 SUM(CASE WHEN p.status IN ('failed', 'refunded') THEN p.amount ELSE 0 END) AS problematic_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND o.order_date < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY u.country
HAVING COUNT(p.payment_id) >= 20
 AND SUM(CASE WHEN p.status IN ('failed', 'refunded') THEN p.amount ELSE 0 END) > SUM(CASE WHEN p.status = 'paid' THEN p.amount ELSE 0 END)
ORDER BY problematic_amount DESC, u.country ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.country,
 SUM(CASE WHEN p.status = 'paid' THEN p.amount ELSE 0 END) AS paid_amount,
 SUM(CASE WHEN p.status IN ('failed', 'refunded') THEN p.amount ELSE 0 END) AS problematic_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND o.order_date >= '2024-01-01'
 AND o.order_date < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY u.country
HAVING COUNT(p.payment_id) >= 20
 AND SUM(CASE WHEN p.status IN ('failed', 'refunded') THEN p.amount ELSE 0 END) > SUM(CASE WHEN p.status = 'paid' THEN p.amount ELSE 0 END)
ORDER BY problematic_amount DESC, u.country ASC;
explanation: The query joins users, orders, and payments so payment rows can be analyzed at the country level. The WHERE clause limits the detail rows to payments tied to completed 2024 orders before any aggregation occurs. Two CASE-based sums then split the grouped payment amount into paid and problematic buckets, and HAVING applies both the minimum-volume rule and the business comparison that problematic_amount must exceed paid_amount. This is more about layered business rules than about introducing a new SQL feature.
hints: First restrict the rows to payments tied to completed orders in 2024.
Use separate CASE-based sums for paid and problematic payment amounts.
The country-level comparison belongs in HAVING because it depends on grouped totals.

title: Quarter-over-quarter revenue change
description:
Compute total `net_amount` per calendar quarter from the `orders` table. Label each quarter as `'YYYY-QN'` (e.g. `'2024-Q1'`). Use LAG() to compute `qoq_change` = current quarter revenue minus the previous quarter. Return `quarter`, `total_revenue` (rounded to 2 decimal places), and `qoq_change` (rounded to 2 decimal places; NULL for the first quarter). Order by `quarter` ascending.

To derive the quarter number: `((CAST(STRFTIME('%m', order_date) AS INT) - 1) / 3 + 1)`.
expected_query:
WITH quarterly AS (
    SELECT STRFTIME('%Y', order_date) || '-Q' ||
           CAST(((CAST(STRFTIME('%m', order_date) AS INT) - 1) / 3 + 1) AS TEXT) AS quarter,
           ROUND(SUM(net_amount), 2) AS total_revenue
    FROM orders
    GROUP BY quarter
)
SELECT quarter,
       total_revenue,
       ROUND(total_revenue - LAG(total_revenue) OVER (ORDER BY quarter), 2) AS qoq_change
FROM quarterly
ORDER BY quarter
solution_query:
WITH quarterly AS (
    SELECT STRFTIME('%Y', order_date) || '-Q' ||
           CAST(((CAST(STRFTIME('%m', order_date) AS INT) - 1) / 3 + 1) AS TEXT) AS quarter,
           ROUND(SUM(net_amount), 2) AS total_revenue
    FROM orders
    GROUP BY quarter
)
SELECT quarter,
       total_revenue,
       ROUND(total_revenue - LAG(total_revenue) OVER (ORDER BY quarter), 2) AS qoq_change
FROM quarterly
ORDER BY quarter;
explanation: A CTE first aggregates revenue per quarter. The quarter label is built by concatenating the year (STRFTIME('%Y')), '-Q', and the quarter number derived from the month. In the outer query, LAG(total_revenue) OVER (ORDER BY quarter) fetches the previous row's revenue — because quarters sort lexicographically in 'YYYY-QN' format, this gives the correct prior quarter. Subtracting gives qoq_change; the first row has no prior, so LAG returns NULL.
hints: First aggregate to quarterly revenue in a CTE, then apply LAG() in the outer SELECT
LAG(col) OVER (ORDER BY quarter) gives you the previous row's value — subtract it from the current row to get the change

title: Full outer join: order-payment reconciliation
description:
Identify mismatches between orders and payments. Perform a FULL OUTER JOIN between `orders` (alias `o`) and `payments` (alias `p`) on `order_id`. Return:
- `order_id`: `COALESCE(o.order_id, p.order_id)`
- `order_amount`: `o.net_amount` (NULL if no order record)
- `payment_amount`: `p.amount` (NULL if no payment record)
- `reconciliation_status`: `'order_only'` if payment is NULL, `'payment_only'` if order is NULL, `'matched'` if both exist

Order by `order_id` ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT COALESCE(o.order_id, p.order_id) AS order_id,
       o.net_amount AS order_amount,
       p.amount AS payment_amount,
       CASE
           WHEN p.order_id IS NULL THEN 'order_only'
           WHEN o.order_id IS NULL THEN 'payment_only'
           ELSE 'matched'
       END AS reconciliation_status
FROM orders o
FULL OUTER JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
ORDER BY order_id
solution_query:
SELECT COALESCE(o.order_id, p.order_id) AS order_id,
       o.net_amount AS order_amount,
       p.amount AS payment_amount,
       CASE
           WHEN p.order_id IS NULL THEN 'order_only'
           WHEN o.order_id IS NULL THEN 'payment_only'
           ELSE 'matched'
       END AS reconciliation_status
FROM orders o
FULL OUTER JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
ORDER BY order_id;
explanation: FULL OUTER JOIN returns all rows from both tables, filling NULL for columns from the table with no match. COALESCE(o.order_id, p.order_id) produces a non-NULL order_id regardless of which side has the match. The CASE expression classifies each row: when p.order_id is NULL, only an order exists; when o.order_id is NULL, only a payment exists; otherwise both match. This reconciliation pattern is essential in financial data pipelines to detect payment gaps or orphaned transactions.
hints: FULL OUTER JOIN keeps all rows from both tables — rows with no match get NULLs on the other side
Use COALESCE(o.order_id, p.order_id) to get a non-null order_id regardless of which side matched

title: Departments with employee count and region
description: For each department, count the number of employees. Include departments even if they have zero employees. Return `department_name`, `region`, and `employee_count`. Order by `employee_count` descending, then `department_name` ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT d.department_name, d.region, COUNT(e.employee_id) AS employee_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
GROUP BY d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region
ORDER BY employee_count DESC, d.department_name ASC
solution_query:
SELECT d.department_name, d.region,
       COUNT(e.employee_id) AS employee_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
GROUP BY d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region
ORDER BY employee_count DESC, d.department_name ASC;
explanation: The JOIN direction matters: LEFT JOIN from departments to employees preserves all department rows even when no employees exist. COUNT(e.employee_id) counts only non-NULL employee IDs — for departments with no employees, this returns 0 (not 1 or NULL). Using COUNT(*) instead would incorrectly count 1 for empty departments because the LEFT JOIN produces a row with NULLs. Grouping by department_id ensures uniqueness even if two departments share the same name.
hints: Join FROM departments TO employees (departments is the left/driving table) so no department is lost
COUNT(e.employee_id) counts non-null values — returns 0 for departments with no employees, unlike COUNT(*)

title: Top revenue by country and plan tier
description: For each combination of user `country` and `plan_tier`, compute the total `net_amount` of completed orders. Return only combinations where total revenue exceeds 5000. Return `country`, `plan_tier`, and `total_revenue` (rounded to 2 decimal places). Order by `total_revenue` descending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.country, u.plan_tier, ROUND(SUM(o.net_amount), 2) AS total_revenue
FROM orders o
JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.user_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY u.country, u.plan_tier
HAVING SUM(o.net_amount) > 5000
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
solution_query:
SELECT u.country, u.plan_tier,
       ROUND(SUM(o.net_amount), 2) AS total_revenue
FROM orders o
JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.user_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY u.country, u.plan_tier
HAVING SUM(o.net_amount) > 5000
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;
explanation: WHERE filters rows before aggregation (only completed orders contribute). GROUP BY produces one row per (country, plan_tier) combination. HAVING filters groups after aggregation — it's the only way to filter on aggregate values like SUM. Using WHERE SUM(...) > 5000 would be a syntax error. The distinction between WHERE (row-level filter) and HAVING (group-level filter) is one of the most commonly tested SQL concepts.
hints: Use WHERE to filter rows before the GROUP BY, and HAVING to filter the aggregated groups after
HAVING filters on the result of the aggregation — WHERE SUM(...) > 5000 is a syntax error
